**[Names]** Golden Ball (金球), Hú Shell (鵠殼).

Li Shizhen states: According to Lu Dian's *Piya* [Supplementary Elegance]: the orange [chéng 橙] belongs to the pomelo [yòu] category. It is said that it 'can be climbed upon to ripen' [a folk-etymological explanation], hence the character uses the element 登 (to ascend). It is also a phonetic compound.

**[Collected Explanations]** [Su] Zhi states: The orange tree resembles the mandarin [jú] but has larger leaves. Its fruit is round, larger than the mandarin and more fragrant, with thick and wrinkled skin; it ripens in the eighth month.

Li Shizhen states: Oranges grow in the southern regions. Their fruit resembles the pomelo and is fragrant; the leaves bear two notches, as if cut into two sections. There is also a variety whose aroma is unpleasant. The pomelo is the largest of the citrus [gān] family, turning yellow early and difficult to keep; the orange is the largest of the mandarin [jú] family, ripening late and lasting well — each has both large and small varieties. According to the *Shilei Hebi* [Classified Compendium of Affairs]: the orange tree has high branches; its leaves do not closely resemble those of the mandarin, and it also bears thorns. Its largest fruits are the size of a bowl, rather like the red luan [zhū luán, i.e., shaddock/pomelo variety]; they ripen early after frost, yellow in colour with thick skin, puckered and ridged like a boiling surface, and richly fragrant. The peel can be used to scent clothing, to wash fresh fish, to mix with pickled vegetables and meat sauces, to make seasoned pastes, to be simmered in honey, to be processed with sugar into orange strips [chéng dīng], or to be preserved with honey into orange paste [chéng gāo]. To smell it is delightful; to eat it is delicious — truly a fine fruit.

[Kou] Zongshi states: Orange peel is today used only as a food, or combined into a drink to serve guests; it is not seen used as medicine. Those who have not yet sobered up from the previous night's drinking will recover quickly upon eating it.

**[Flavour and Nature]** Sour, cold, non-toxic.

[Meng] Shiliang states: [Also considered] warm. Excessive consumption injures the liver qi and brings on deficiency heat. Eaten together with otter flesh [獺肉], it causes dizziness and nausea.

Li Shizhen states: The character [獺] refers to the river otter [water otter] family. All other materia medica works write it as 'areca nut' [檳榔], which is an error.
[The peel is:] bitter, acrid, warm, non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**

*Fruit:* Wash away the sour juice, slice and combine with salt and honey, simmer and store for eating; [this] stops nausea and can expel the floating wind-evil qi from the stomach (Kaibao [Bencao]).

Promotes the movement of wind qi, treats goitre [yǐng qi], disperses scrofula [luǒ lì], and neutralises the toxicity of fish and crab (Shiliang).

*Peel:* Made into sauce or vinegar it is fragrant and pleasant; disperses evil qi from the intestines and stomach, aids digestion and directs qi downward, expels floating wind qi from the stomach (Kaibao [Bencao]). Preserved with salt and eaten, it stops nausea and relieves alcohol sickness (Meng Shen). Made with sugar into orange strips [chéng dīng], it is sweet and pleasant, dissolves phlegm and directs qi downward, benefits the diaphragm and relaxes the middle [burner], and relieves alcohol [intoxication] (Li Shizhen).

*For facial blemishes and acne [粉刺]:* Grind [the peel] while moist and apply nightly (Li Shizhen).

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus sinensis (sweet orange) or Citrus aurantium (bitter/sour orange)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the largest of the jú [mandarin] family, late-ripening, thick-skinned, and very fragrant. Both sweet and bitter orange species were known in Ming-dynasty China; the text likely encompasses both.

entities_mentioned.name: 柚 (yòu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus maxima (pomelo)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the largest of the gān [citrus/mandarin] family, turning yellow early and difficult to keep.

entities_mentioned.name: 朱欒 (zhū luán)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus maxima or a large pomelo/shaddock variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparison for the largest oranges.

entities_mentioned.name: 橙丁 (chéng dīng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Candied/sugar-preserved orange peel strips
entities_mentioned.notes: A preparation method: sugar-processing the peel into small strips.

entities_mentioned.name: 橙膏 (chéng gāo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey-preserved orange paste
entities_mentioned.notes: A preparation method: honey-preserving the peel into a paste.

entities_mentioned.name: 獺 (tǎ) / 水獺
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lutra lutra (Eurasian river otter)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen corrects earlier sources that mistakenly wrote 'areca nut' (檳榔) instead of 'otter' (獺) as the contraindicated food combination with orange.

entities_mentioned.name: 癭氣 (yǐng qì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Goitre (thyroid enlargement)
entities_mentioned.notes: Condition treated by the fruit of the orange.

entities_mentioned.name: 瘰癧 (luǒ lì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scrofula (tuberculous cervical lymphadenitis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Condition for which the orange fruit is said to help disperse.

results.translator_notes: 1. The character rendered as '㰼' (wash/rinse fresh food) in the phrase '可以㰼鮮' is an uncommon character meaning to rinse or clean fresh fish/meat; translated contextually. 2. Li Shizhen's philological note correcting 獺 (otter) vs. 檳榔 (areca nut) is an important textual-critical remark preserved in translation. 3. The phrase '蹙衄如沸' (literally 'puckered and bleeding like boiling') is a vivid description of the orange peel's dimpled, bubbled surface texture — translated as 'puckered and ridged like a boiling surface.' 4. The character '面㾴粉刺' involves '㾴' (facial blemish/dark spot); OCR originally rendered this unclearly. 5. The two flavour entries (酸寒 for the fruit and 苦辛溫 for the peel) are distinguished in the original but run together; the translation explicitly labels which part is described.

【釋名】（與柚同）、條（《爾雅》）、壺柑（《唐本》）、臭橙（《食性》）、朱欒。

時珍曰︰柚，色油然，其狀如卣，故名。壺亦象形。今人呼其黃而小者為蜜筒，正此意也。其大者謂之朱欒，亦取團欒之象。最大者謂之香欒。《爾雅》謂之𣔼（音廢），又曰櫾（音賈）。《廣雅》謂之鐳柚，鐳亦壺也。《桂海志》謂之臭柚，皆一物。但以大小、古今方言稱呼不同耳。

【集解】恭曰︰柚皮濃味甘，不似橘皮薄味辛而苦。其肉亦如橘，有甘有酸，酸者名壺柑。今俗人謂橙為柚，非矣。案︰《呂氏春秋》云︰果之美者，江浦之橘，云夢之柚。郭璞云︰柚出江南，似橙而實酢，大如橘。禹貢云︰揚州厥包橘、柚。孔安國云︰小曰橘，大曰柚，皆為柑也。

頌曰︰閩中、嶺外、江南皆有柚，比橘黃，白色而大。襄、唐間柚，色青黃而實小。其味皆酢，皮濃，不堪入藥。

時珍曰︰柚，樹、葉皆似橙。其實有大、小二種︰小者如柑、如橙；大者如瓜、如升，有圍及尺餘者，亦橙之類也。今人呼為朱欒，形色圓正，都類柑、橙。但皮濃而粗，其味甘，其氣臭，其瓣堅而酸惡不可食，其花甚香。南人種其核，長成以接柑、橘，云甚良也。蓋橙乃橘屬，故其皮皺濃而香，味苦而辛；柚乃柑屬，故其皮粗濃而臭，味甘而辛。如此分，柚與橙、橘自明矣。郭璞云︰櫾，大柚也。實大如盞，皮濃二、三寸，子似枳，食之少味。

范成大云︰廣南臭柚大如瓜，可食，其皮甚濃，染墨打碑，可代氈刷，且不損紙也。《列子》云︰吳越之間有木焉，其名為櫾。碧樹而冬青，實丹而味酸。食其皮汁，已憤厥之疾。渡淮而北，化而為枳。此言地氣之不同如此。

【氣味】酸，寒，無毒。
甘、辛，平，無毒。

【主治】消食，解酒毒，治飲酒人口氣，去腸胃中惡氣，療妊婦不思食、口淡（大明）。

皮
下氣。宜食，不入藥（弘景）。消食快膈，散憤懣之氣，化痰（時珍）。
頭風痛，同蔥白搗，貼太陽穴（時珍）。

花
蒸麻油作香澤面脂，長發潤燥（時珍）。

**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Tiáo [條] (Erya); Hú gān [壺柑, 'jar-orange'] (Tang Materia Medica); Chòu chéng [臭橙, 'stinking orange'] (Shí Xìng [Food Properties]); Zhūluán [朱欒].

Li Shizhen says: The yòu [柚] has an oily [lustrous] appearance and its shape resembles a yǒu [卣, a type of ancient bronze vessel], hence the name. Hú [壺, 'jar'] is likewise a reference to its shape. People today call the smaller, yellow variety mì tǒng ['honey tube'], which carries the same sense. The larger variety is called zhūluán, also evoking its round, full shape. The very largest is called xiāng luán ['fragrant luán']. The Erya refers to it as 𣔼 (pronounced fèi) and also as 櫾 (pronounced jiǎ). The Guangya calls it léi yòu [鐳柚]—léi likewise meaning a jar or vessel. The Guìhǎi Zhì calls it chòu yòu ['stinking yòu']. All these names refer to the same fruit; the differences are merely due to size, era, and regional dialect.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Su Gong says: The yòu has a thick rind with a sweet flavour, unlike the thin, pungent, and bitter rind of the orange [júzi]. Its flesh is also like the orange, being either sweet or sour; the sour variety is called hú gān. Common people today call the orange a yòu, which is incorrect. According to the Lüshi Chunqiu: 'Among fine fruits are the oranges of Jiangpu and the yòu of Yunmeng.' Guo Pu says: 'The yòu comes from Jiangnan; it resembles the orange but its flesh is sour, and it is as large as an orange.' The Tribute of Yu [Shujing] states: 'The tribute of Yangzhou includes packages of oranges and yòu.' Kong Anguo comments: 'The small one is called jú [橘], the large one is called yòu [柚]; both are types of gān [柑, mandarin-orange].'

Su Song says: The yòu is found in Minzhong, beyond the Nanling mountains, and throughout Jiangnan. It is larger than the orange, with a yellowish-white colour. The yòu of the Xiang and Tang regions is greenish-yellow and produces smaller fruits. All varieties are sour-tasting with a thick rind and are not suitable for medicinal use.

Li Shizhen says: The yòu tree and its leaves both resemble the orange tree. The fruit comes in two sizes: the smaller kind resembles the gān [柑] or the orange; the larger kind resembles a melon or a shēng [升, a dry measure], with a circumference exceeding a foot—this too belongs to the orange family. People today call it zhūluán; it is round and regular in shape, much resembling the gān and the orange, but with a thick, coarse rind. Its flavour is sweet, its odour is pungent [lit. 'stinking'], its segments are firm and unpleasantly sour and inedible, yet its flowers are very fragrant. Southerners plant its seeds and grow the stock for grafting onto gān and jú, which is said to work very well. Now, the orange [chéng, 橙] belongs to the jú [橘] family—hence its rind is wrinkled, thick, and fragrant, with a bitter and pungent taste. The yòu belongs to the gān [柑] family—hence its rind is coarse, thick, and malodorous, with a sweet and pungent taste. By this distinction, the yòu, the orange [chéng], and the jú [橘] are readily told apart. Guo Pu says: 'The 櫾 is the large yòu. Its fruit is as large as a cup, its rind is two to three cun [inches] thick, its seeds resemble those of zhǐ [枳, trifoliate orange], and it has little flavour when eaten.'

Fan Chengda says: 'The stinking yòu [chòu yòu] of Guangnan is as large as a melon and is edible; its rind is very thick, and when soaked in ink and pressed on a stone inscription rubbing, it can substitute for a felt brush and does not damage the paper.' The Liezi states: 'Between Wu and Yue there is a tree called 櫾 [yòu]. Its leaves are deep green and evergreen in winter; its fruit is red and sour in flavour. Eating the juice of its rind cures the ailment of fènjué [憤厥, fainting from stifled rage/qi obstruction]. But once it crosses the Huai River northward, it transforms into zhǐ [枳].' This illustrates how greatly the character of the earth's qi may differ by region.

**[Qi and Flavour]**
Sour, cold, non-toxic.
Sweet and pungent, balanced [平], non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
*(Flesh)*
Promotes digestion; resolves alcohol toxicity; treats bad breath in drinkers; expels noxious qi from the stomach and intestines; treats loss of appetite and bland taste in the mouth in pregnant women. (Da Ming)

*(Rind)*
Directs qi downward [descends rebellious qi]. Suitable as food; not used in medicine. (Hongjing [Tao Hongjing])
Promotes digestion, clears and opens the diaphragm, disperses pent-up and depressed qi [fèn mèn], and resolves phlegm. (Li Shizhen)
[For] wind headache [tóufēng tòng]: pound the rind together with scallion-white [cōng bái] and apply as a poultice to the tàiyáng acupoint [temples]. (Li Shizhen)

*(Flower)*
Steam with sesame oil to make a fragrant facial ointment [xiāng zé miàn zhī]: promotes hair growth and moistens dryness. (Li Shizhen)

results.translator_notes:
1. Several characters in the original appear to have been corrupted or are rare graphemes (e.g., 𣔼, 櫾); these have been restored based on context and standard references to the Erya and Guangya. They are marked with their reconstructed readings in the translation.
2. The 'Qi and Flavour' section lists two sets of properties without specifying which part of the fruit each set applies to; the first (sour, cold) likely refers to the sour-fleshed variety or the flesh generally, the second (sweet-pungent, balanced) likely to the sweet variety. The original text is ambiguous.
3. 'Fènjué' (憤厥) is rendered as 'fainting from stifled rage/qi obstruction,' a condition associated in classical Chinese medicine with extreme emotional suppression causing qi reversal.
4. 'Tàiyáng acupoint' refers to the temporal region, a classical acupuncture/acupressure point for headache.
5. The Liezi passage about the tree transforming into zhǐ north of the Huai River is a well-known classical allusion; it is faithfully reproduced here as Li Shizhen cites it to discuss the role of regional soil and climate.

【釋名】香櫞（俗作圓）、佛手柑。

時珍曰︰義未詳。佛手，取象也。

【集解】藏器曰︰枸櫞生嶺南，柑、橘之屬也。其葉大，其實大如盞，味辛酸。

頌曰︰今閩廣、江南皆有之，彼人呼為香櫞子。形長如小瓜狀，其皮若橙而光澤可愛，肉甚濃，白如蘿蔔而松虛。雖味短而香芬大勝，置衣笥中，則數日香不歇。寄至北方，人甚貴重。古作五和糝用之。

時珍曰︰枸櫞產閩廣間。木似朱欒而葉尖長，枝間有刺。植之近水乃生。其實狀如人手，有指，俗呼為佛手柑。有長一尺四、五寸者。皮如橙柚而濃，皺而光澤。其色如瓜，生綠熟黃。其核細。其味不甚佳而清香襲人。南人雕鏤花鳥，作蜜煎果食。置之几案，可供玩。若安芋片於蒂而以濕紙圍護，經久不癟。或搗蒜罨其蒂上，則香更充溢。《異物志》云：浸汁浣葛，勝似酸漿也！

皮瓤

【氣味】辛、酸，無毒。弘景曰︰性溫。恭曰︰性冷。陶說誤矣。藏器曰︰性溫

【主治】下氣，除心頭痰水（藏器）。煮酒飲，治痰氣咳嗽。煎湯，治心下氣痛（時珍）。

根、葉
同皮（《橘譜》）。
results.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Also called Xiang Yuan (commonly written as 圓, 'round'); and Fo Shou Gan [Buddha's Hand Citrus].

Li Shizhen says: The precise meaning of the name 枸櫞 (Gou Yuan) is not entirely clear. The name 'Buddha's Hand' (Fo Shou) is derived from its resemblance in shape.

**[Collected Explanations]** Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] says: Gou Yuan grows in Lingnan [southern China, south of the Nanling Mountains]. It belongs to the mandarin-orange and tangerine [citrus] family. Its leaves are large, its fruit is as large as a small cup, and its flavor is pungent and sour.

Su Song [Song] says: It is found today in Fujian, Guangdong, and Jiangnan, where the locals call it Xiang Yuan Zi ['fragrant citron fruit']. It is elongated in shape, resembling a small melon. Its rind is like that of an orange but glossy and pleasing to the eye. The flesh is very thick, white like a radish, yet spongy and loose. Although its flavor is mild, its fragrance is greatly superior; when placed inside a clothes chest, the scent lingers for several days without fading. When sent to the northern regions, people hold it in high regard. In ancient times it was used as one of the ingredients in the Five-Harmony Relish [wu he san].

Li Shizhen says: Gou Yuan is produced in the Fujian and Guangdong region. The tree resembles the Zhu Luan [pomelo] but has pointed, elongated leaves, and spines grow between the branches. It grows best when planted near water. The fruit is shaped like a human hand, with finger-like projections, and is commonly called Fo Shou Gan [Buddha's Hand Citrus]. Some specimens reach one chi four or five cun [approximately 45–50 cm] in length. The rind is like that of an orange or citron but thicker, wrinkled, and glossy. In color it resembles a melon: green when unripe, yellow when ripe. The seeds are small. The flavor is not particularly fine, but the clear fragrance is captivating. People of the south carve flowers and birds into its surface and prepare it as a candied confection. Placed on a table or desk, it can serve as an ornament. If a slice of taro is placed at the stem end and wrapped with damp paper, the fruit will remain plump for a long time without shriveling. Alternatively, pounding garlic and applying it over the stem end will make the fragrance even more abundant. The Yiwu Zhi [Record of Strange Things] states: 'Soaking [the fruit] and using the juice to wash kudzu cloth is superior to using sour slurry [suan jiang]!'

**Rind and Flesh [Pi Rang]**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Pungent, sour; non-toxic. Tao Hongjing says: Warm in nature. Su Gong [Kong] says: Cold in nature; Tao's account is mistaken. Chen Cangqi says: Warm in nature.

**[Indications and Treatment]** Descends qi [regulates the flow of vital energy downward]; eliminates phlegm and water accumulation around the heart [epigastric region] (Chen Cangqi). Simmered in wine and drunk, it treats cough due to phlegm-qi obstruction. Decocted into a soup, it treats qi pain below the heart [epigastric pain] (Li Shizhen).

**Root and Leaves**
Same [therapeutic use] as the rind (as recorded in the Ju Pu [Treatise on Citrus]).

results.translator_notes:
1. The text conflates two closely related plants — the common citron (Citrus medica) and the Buddha's Hand cultivar (Citrus medica var. sarcodactylis) — treating them under a single entry, which reflects the period understanding. Modern botany distinguishes them as varieties of the same species.
2. 'Heart area' (心頭, 心下) in therapeutic indications refers to the epigastric region, not the cardiac organ per se, consistent with classical Chinese medical terminology.
3. The disagreement between Tao Hongjing (warm) and Su Gong (cold) regarding thermal nature reflects a common pattern of doctrinal dispute in the Bencao tradition; Li Shizhen does not resolve it explicitly here.
4. The citation from 《異物志》 (Yiwu Zhi, 'Record of Strange Things') refers likely to the text attributed to Yang Fu of the Han/Three Kingdoms period, concerning wonders of the southern regions.
5. The OCR rendered '-{几}-案' and '-{云}-' with formatting artifacts; these have been silently corrected to 几案 and 云 respectively, as the intended characters are unambiguous in context.

【釋名】鶯桃（《禮注》）、含桃（《月令》）、荊桃。

宗奭曰︰孟詵《本草》言︰此乃櫻，非桃也。雖非桃類，以其形肖桃，故曰櫻桃，又何疑焉？如沐猴梨、胡桃之類，皆取其形相似耳。《禮記》︰仲春，天子以含桃荐宗廟，即此。故王維詩云︰才是寢園春荐後，非幹御苑鳥銜殘。藥中不甚用。

時珍曰︰其顆如瓔珠，故謂之櫻。而許慎作鶯桃，雲鶯所含食，故又曰含桃，亦通。案︰《爾雅》云︰楔（音戛），荊桃也。孫炎注云︰即今櫻桃。最大而甘者，謂之崖蜜。

【集解】頌曰︰櫻桃，處處有之，而洛中者最勝。其木多陰，先百果熟，故古人多貴之。其實熟時深紅色者，謂之朱櫻。紫色，皮裡有細黃點者，謂之紫櫻，味最珍重。又有正黃明者，謂之蠟櫻。小而紅者，謂之櫻珠，味皆不及。極大者，有若彈丸，核細而肉濃，尤難得。

時珍曰︰櫻桃樹不甚高。春初開白花，繁英如雪。葉團，有尖及細齒。結子一枝數十顆，三月熟時須守護，否則鳥食無遺也。鹽藏、蜜煎皆可，或同蜜搗作糕食，唐人以酪荐食之。林洪《山家清供》云︰櫻桃經雨則蟲自內生，人莫之見。用水浸良久，則蟲皆出，乃可食也。試之果然。

【氣味】甘，熱，澀，無毒。大明曰︰平，微毒。多食令人吐。

詵曰︰食多無損

**Names and Synonyms**
Also known as: Zhū Táo [Red Peach] (Bielu), Mài Yīng [Wheat Cherry] (Wu Pu), Yīng Dòu [Heroic Bean] (Bielu), and Lǐ Táo [Plum-Peach].

Meng Shen states: This yīng táo is commonly called Lǐ Táo, and also Nài Táo. The previously discussed cherry (yīngtáo) is named yīng, not a peach at all.

**Collected Explanations**
The Bielu states: The fruit of yīng táo is as large as a grain of wheat and heavily covered with hair. It is gathered in the fourth month and dried in the shade.

Tao Hongjing states: The cherry (yīngtáo) is what we now call the red cherry (zhū yīng), which can be cooked and eaten. The mountain cherry (yīng táo) resembles it in form but differs in substance; it is found from time to time in the hills, and is not used in prescriptions or medicines.

Li Shizhen states: The tree resembles the red cherry, but its leaves are long and pointed rather than rounded. The fruit is small and pointed; it is green when unripe and turns yellow-red when ripe. It also lacks luster and has an unpleasant flavor, making it unfit to eat.

**Fruit**

*Flavor and Nature:* Acrid [xīn], neutral [píng], non-toxic.

*Indications and Therapeutic Uses:* Stops diarrhea and intestinal dysentery [cháng pì]; eliminates heat; regulates the middle [jiāo] and supplements spleen qi; improves complexion; uplifts the spirit (Bielu). Stops seminal emission [xiè jīng] (Meng Shen).

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text reads '腸' followed by a gap that appears to be an OCR error; contextually this is almost certainly '腸澼' (cháng pì), a classical term for intestinal dysentery or bloody flux, and has been restored as such in both the cleaned text and translation.
2. '美志' is rendered as 'uplifts the spirit' — literally 'beautifies the will/aspiration'; it may refer to a general sense of emotional well-being.
3. Meng Shen (孟詵) refers to the Tang-dynasty physician and author of the Shiliao Bencao (Dietary Materia Medica). Tao Hongjing (弘景) refers to the Liang-dynasty scholar Tao Hongjing, commentator on the Shennong Bencao Jing.
4. '柰桃' (Nài Táo) is an alternate folk name; 'nài' normally refers to a type of apple (Malus), suggesting possible popular confusion or broad use of fruit names in vernacular speech.

【釋名】白果（《日用》）、鴨腳子。

時珍曰︰原生江南，葉似鴨掌，因名鴨腳。宋初始入貢，改呼銀杏，因其形似小杏而核色白也。今名白果。梅堯臣詩︰鴨腳類綠李，其名因葉高。歐陽修詩︰絳囊初入貢，銀杏貴中州。是矣。

【集解】時珍曰︰銀杏生江南，以宣城者為勝。樹高二、三丈。葉薄縱理，儼如鴨掌形，有刻缺，面綠背淡。二月開花成簇，青白色，二更開花，隨即卸落，人罕見之。一枝結子百十，狀如楝子，經霜乃熟爛。去肉取核為果，其核兩頭尖，三棱為雄，二棱為雌。其仁嫩時綠色，久則黃。須雌雄同種，其樹相望，乃結實；或雌樹臨水亦可；或鑿一孔，內雄木一塊，泥之，亦結。陰陽相感之妙如此。其樹耐久，肌理白膩。術家取刻符印，云能召使也。

《文選‧吳都賦》注︰平仲果，其實如銀。未知即此果否？

核仁

瑞曰︰多食壅氣動風。小兒食多昏霍，發驚引疳。同鰻鱺魚食，患軟風。

【主治】生食，引疳解酒，熟食益人（李鵬飛）。熟食，溫肺益氣，定喘嗽，縮小便，止白濁。生食，降痰，消毒殺蟲。嚼漿，塗鼻面手足，去 皴皺，及疥癬疳 陰虱（時珍）。

**Names and Synonyms**
Also known as: Húzhēnzǐ ["foreign hazelnut"] (from *Shiyi* [Supplement to Materia Medica]), and Wúmíngzǐ ["nameless seed"] (from *Haiyao* [Overseas Materia Medica]).

**Collected Explanations**
Chen Cangqi states: Āyuèhúnzǐ grows in the various foreign countries of the West. It shares the same tree as Húzhēnzǐ [the foreign hazelnut]: in the first year the tree produces Húzhēnzǐ, and in the second year it produces Āyuèhúnzǐ.
[Another commentator] notes: According to Xu Biao's *Nanzhou Ji* [Record of the Southern Regions]: the Wúmíng tree grows in the mountain valleys of Lingnan [southern China]. Its fruit resembles a hazelnut in appearance and is called Wúmíngzǐ ["nameless seed"]. The Persian people call it Āyuèhúnzǐ.

**Kernel (Rén)**

*Flavor and Nature:* Pungent, warm, astringent, non-toxic. [Another source:] Pungent, greatly warm, non-toxic.

*Principal Treatments:* Treats all manner of dysentery [zhūlì]; dispels cold qi [cold vital energy]; makes a person well-nourished and robust (Chen Cangqi). Treats cold sensation in the lower back and deficiency-weakness of the yin kidneys [reproductive/urological weakness]; it is frequently used in the arts of the bedchamber [fángzhōng shù — sexual cultivation practices]. It works especially well in combination with mù xiāng [costus root] and shān zhūyú [Cornus officinalis] (Li Xun).

**Bark of the Wúmíng Tree** (from *Haiyao*)
For wilting weakness of the yin kidneys [genital debility] and damp itching beneath the scrotum: decoct the bark into a liquid and use it as a sitz bath [small bath of the affected area] — this is extremely effective.

entities_mentioned.name: 阿月渾子 (Āyuèhúnzǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia vera (pistachio)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 阿月渾子 is a transliteration of the Persian word for pistachio (pistah / āl-ūzun). The text explicitly notes that Persians call it by this name. The description of it as a hazelnut-like fruit from western foreign lands is consistent with pistachio.

entities_mentioned.name: 胡榛子 (Húzhēnzǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia vera or a related Pistacia species
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as coming from the same tree as 阿月渾子, produced in the first year. May refer to a related Pistacia species fruit, or alternatively could be a confusion between two stages of the same plant's fruiting cycle.

entities_mentioned.name: 無名子 / 無名木 (Wúmíngzǐ / Wúmíng tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia vera (pistachio)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified in the text itself as the same plant as Āyuèhúnzǐ; the name means 'nameless seed/tree.' Said to grow in Lingnan (southern China) according to Xu Biao's Nanzhou Ji, though the pistachio is not native to Lingnan — this may reflect trade goods or a misidentification of a related species.

entities_mentioned.name: 木香 (Mù xiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saussurea costus (costus root / Indian costus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with 阿月渾子 kernel to treat kidney deficiency and lower back cold.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (Shān zhūyú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis (Japanese cornelian cherry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with 阿月渾子 kernel; a classical tonic herb for kidney and liver deficiency.

The attribution '（李 ）' in the source text has a missing character after 李, almost certainly 李珣 (Li Xun), the Tang-dynasty author of *Haiyao Bencao* — corrected silently in the cleaned text. 2. The claim that Āyuèhúnzǐ and Húzhēnzǐ come from 'the same tree' in different years is botanically incorrect and likely reflects a misunderstanding or folk belief. 3. 'Fángzhōng shù' (房中術) refers to classical Chinese sexual cultivation/longevity practices; rendered transparently with a bracketed gloss. 4. The 'small bath' (小浴) for the bark decoction is a sitz bath or local wash application, not a full bath. 5. Several attribution markers at the start of quotations are unclear or abbreviated in the original, likely due to OCR loss or manuscript damage.

results.title_inferred: Zhuzhi (Chu Fruit) — Stone Oak / Evergreen Oak Nut
results.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰櫧子生江南。皮、樹如栗，冬月不凋，子小於橡子。

穎曰︰櫧子有苦、甜二種，治作粉食、糕食，褐色甚佳。

時珍曰︰櫧子，處處山谷有之。其木大者數抱，高二、三丈。葉長大如栗，葉稍尖而濃堅光澤，鋸齒峭利，凌冬不凋。三、四月開白花成穗，如栗花。結實大如槲子，外有小苞，霜後苞裂子墜。子圓褐而有尖，大如菩提子。內仁如杏仁，生食苦澀，煮、炒乃帶甘，亦可磨粉。甜櫧子粒小，木紋細白，俗名面櫧。苦櫧子粒大，木紋粗赤，俗名血櫧。其色黑者名鐵櫧。按︰《山海經》云︰前山有木，其名曰櫧。郭璞注曰︰櫧子似柞子，可食，冬月採之。

木作屋柱、棺材，難腐也。

仁

【氣味】苦、澀，平，無毒。

時珍曰︰案︰《正要》云︰酸、甘，微寒。不可多食。

【主治】食之不飢，令人健行，止泄痢，破惡血，止渴（藏器）。

皮、葉
煮汁飲，止產婦血（藏器）。嫩葉︰貼 瘡，一日三換，良（吳瑞）。
results.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]**

Zangqi [Chen Cangqi] states: Zhuzhi [chu fruit] grows in Jiangnan. The bark and tree resemble chestnut [Castanea]; it does not shed its leaves in winter, and the fruit is smaller than acorns [xiangzi].

Ying [Hua Shou] states: Zhuzhi comes in two varieties — bitter and sweet. The fruit can be processed into flour for eating or made into cakes; it is brownish in color and quite fine.

Shizhen [Li Shizhen] states: Zhuzhi is found in mountain valleys everywhere. The larger trees require several arms' spans to encircle and stand two to three zhang [approximately 6–10 meters] tall. The leaves are long and large, like chestnut leaves, but slightly more pointed, thick, firm, and glossy, with sharp serrated edges; they do not fall in winter. In the third and fourth months [April–May], white flowers bloom in spikes, resembling chestnut flowers. The fruit is as large as a Quercus dentata [huzhi] acorn, enclosed in a small involucre [bract cup]; after frost the involucre splits and the nut falls. The nut is round and brownish with a pointed tip, about the size of a bodhi seed [Elaeocarpus robustus]. The inner kernel resembles an apricot kernel; eaten raw it is bitter and astringent, but becomes mildly sweet when boiled or roasted; it can also be ground into flour. The sweet variety [tian zhuzhi] has smaller nuts and fine, white-grained wood, commonly called 'mian zhu' [flour chu]. The bitter variety [ku zhuzhi] has larger nuts and coarse, red-grained wood, commonly called 'xue zhu' [blood chu]. The variety with black-colored wood is called 'tie zhu' [iron chu]. Note: The Shanhaijing [Classic of Mountains and Seas] states: 'On the Qian Mountains there is a tree called zhu.' Guo Pu's commentary notes: 'The zhu fruit resembles the zuozi [Quercus acorn] and is edible; it is gathered in the winter months.'

The wood is used to make house pillars and coffins, as it is highly resistant to decay.

**Kernel [Ren]**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Bitter, astringent; balanced [ping]; non-toxic.

Shizhen states: According to the Zhengyao [Yinshan Zhengyao / Principles of Correct Diet], it is described as sour and sweet, slightly cold [wei han]. It should not be eaten in excess.

**[Indications and Uses]** Eating the fruit wards off hunger, enables vigorous walking [strengthens the body], stops diarrhea and dysentery [xie li], breaks up stagnant [malign] blood [e xue], and relieves thirst. (Zangqi)

**Bark and Leaves**

Boil and drink the decoction to stop postpartum bleeding in women. (Zangqi)

Tender leaves: Apply as a poultice to sores and ulcers [chuang], replacing the dressing three times a day; effective. (Wu Rui)

entities_mentioned.name: 櫧子 (Zhuzhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Castanopsis species, likely Castanopsis sclerophylla (Chinese evergreen chinkapin) or related Castanopsis spp.; possibly also encompassing Lithocarpus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The text describes an evergreen oak-like tree with edible nuts, two varieties (bitter/sweet), found widely in southern Chinese mountain valleys. Castanopsis sclerophylla is the most commonly cited modern identification. The 'sweet' (面櫧/mian zhu) and 'bitter' (血櫧/xue zhu) varieties may represent distinct species within Castanopsis or Lithocarpus.

entities_mentioned.name: 橡子 (Xiangzi)

entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparison reference.

entities_mentioned.name: 槲子 (Huzhi)

entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparison for the fruit.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercus mongolica or related oak species acorn

results.translator_notes: 1. The medical indications extracted: (a) Kernel — relieves hunger, aids walking/strengthens body, stops diarrhea and dysentery, resolves blood stasis, relieves thirst; preparation: consumed directly, boiled, roasted, or ground into flour. (b) Bark and leaves — decoction drunk to stop postpartum hemorrhage. (c) Tender leaves — applied topically as poultice for sores, changed three times daily. 2. The source '《正要》' refers to the Yinshan Zhengyao (飲膳正要), a 14th-century dietary manual by Hu Sihui. 3. The original text has a minor spacing issue around '貼 瘡' (a space between the two characters), likely an OCR artifact; it has been preserved in the original_text and rendered as '貼瘡' in translation. 4. Heights given in zhang: 1 zhang ≈ 3.2 meters in Ming dynasty measure, so 2–3 zhang ≈ 6.4–9.6 meters.

results.title_inferred: Hook Chestnut (Gou Li) — Fruit Chapter Two
results.original_text:
【釋名】巢鉤子（《拾遺》）、甜櫧子。

瑞曰︰鉤栗即甜櫧子。

時珍曰︰鉤、櫧二字，方音相近。其狀如櫟，當作鉤櫟。

【集解】藏器曰︰鉤栗生江南山谷。木大數圍，冬月不凋，其子似栗而圓小。又有雀子，相似而圓黑，久食不飢。詳櫧子下。

仁

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】食之不飢，濃腸胃，令人肥健（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** Also called Cháo Gōu Zǐ (from the *Shiyi* [*Bencao Shiyi*]) and Tián Zhū Zǐ [Sweet Zhū Nut].

Rui [Wu Rui] states: Gōu Lì is the same as Tián Zhū Zǐ.

Shizhen [Li Shizhen] states: The characters *gōu* and *zhū* are phonetically close in regional dialects. Its appearance resembles that of the oak (*Quercus*), so it should properly be written as Gōu Lì [Hook Oak].

**Collected Explanations:** Zang Qi [Chen Cangqi] states: Gōu Lì grows in the mountain valleys of Jiangnan [south of the Yangtze River]. The tree is large, several arm-spans in girth, and does not shed its leaves in winter. Its fruits resemble chestnuts but are round and small. There is also a variety called Què Zǐ [Sparrow Nut], which is similarly shaped but round and black; eating it over a long period prevents hunger. See further details under the entry for Zhū Zǐ.

**Kernel (Rén)**

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet, neutral [平], non-toxic.

**Medicinal Uses:** Eating it prevents hunger, fortifies and thickens the intestines and stomach, and causes a person to become plump and robust. (Zang Qi)

entities_mentioned.name: 鉤栗 (Gōu Lì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Castanopsis eyrei or a related Castanopsis species (evergreen chinkapin); possibly Cyclobalanopsis or Lithocarpus species
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen equates it with Tián Zhū Zǐ (sweet zhū nut) and notes resemblance to oak. The evergreen habit, large trunk, and small round nut-like fruit are consistent with Castanopsis or Lithocarpus (stone oak) of southern China.

entities_mentioned.name: 甜櫧子 (Tián Zhū Zǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Castanopsis species, likely Castanopsis sclerophylla or C. eyrei
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified by multiple sources as synonymous with Gōu Lì; the 'sweet' qualifier distinguishes it from bitter varieties.

entities_mentioned.name: 雀子 (Què Zǐ / Sparrow Nut)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a different Castanopsis or Lithocarpus variety with dark, rounded fruits; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as similar to Gōu Lì but round and black; said to stave off hunger when eaten regularly. Likely a related mast-producing tree species.

results.translator_notes: The condition treated is general debility / poor nourishment, addressed by consuming the kernel. Preparation method is simply eating the kernel (食之). The term 濃腸胃 ('thickens/fortifies the intestines and stomach') is a classical Chinese dietary therapy expression indicating a tonic effect on the digestive system. 'Cháo Gōu Zǐ' (巢鉤子) as listed in the *Bencao Shiyi* (陳藏器, Tang dynasty) is the earlier name. The cross-reference 'see Zhū Zǐ below' indicates a related main entry in the same chapter that provides further detail.
